title: What vibe coding is (and is not)
summary: Vibe coding is building software by describing intent to a model and steering its output, while staying responsible for the result.
body_html:
Vibe coding is writing software mostly by describing what you want to an AI model and iterating on what it produces, rather than typing every line yourself. The term was popularized in 2025 to name a real shift: for many tasks, the bottleneck moved from typing code to specifying intent, reviewing output, and steering the next step.

What it is not is abandoning judgment. The model writes fast and plausibly, but it does not own your product's contract, your edge cases, or your release risk. Productive vibe coding keeps a human responsible for the design, the review, and the acceptance criteria. The model is a fast collaborator, not the final authority.

The skill that pays off is steering: stating intent precisely, working in small reviewable units, and verifying results instead of trusting them. The rest of this track is those skills in detail. Done well, vibe coding makes you faster without making the result less yours.
key_points: Vibe coding means describing intent and steering AI output, not typing every line.
The bottleneck shifts from typing to specifying, reviewing, and steering.
You stay responsible for design, review, and acceptance; the model is a collaborator.
The core skill is steering: precise intent, small units, and verification.

links.title: Karpathy: Vibe Coding MenuGen
links.note: The case study that named vibe coding, with its honest tradeoffs.

links.title: DeepLearning.AI: Prompt Engineering for Developers
links.note: The foundational course on building with LLMs.

flashcards.front: What is vibe coding?
flashcards.back: Building software by describing intent to a model and steering its output, while owning the result.

flashcards.front: What shifts when you vibe code?
flashcards.back: The bottleneck moves from typing code to specifying intent, reviewing, and steering.

flashcards.front: What does vibe coding NOT mean?
flashcards.back: It does not mean handing over judgment; you stay responsible for design and review.

quiz.q: What is the central skill of productive vibe coding?
choices: Steering: precise intent, small reviewable units, and verification.
Trusting the model's output without review.
Avoiding tests.
Typing faster than the model.
quiz.explain: Steering and verification are what keep speed from costing correctness.

quiz.q: Who owns the product contract and release risk when vibe coding?
choices: The model.
The human; the model is a fast collaborator, not the final authority.
Nobody.
The user of the app.
quiz.explain: The model writes plausibly but does not own correctness or risk.

exercise.prompt: You accept an AI-written patch without reading it because it 'looks right'. What responsibility did you skip?
choices: Writing the prompt.
Choosing the model.
None; the model owns correctness.
Review and verification; you still own the result.
exercise.explain: Vibe coding speeds you up but keeps design, review, and acceptance human-owned.

outcome: You can describe what vibe coding is and where human responsibility stays.

title: The coding-tool landscape
summary: Coding tools differ by how you interact with them: inline completion, chat, AI-first editors, and autonomous CLI agents.
body_html:
The tools change fast, but the categories are stable. Sort them by interaction model rather than by brand. Inline completion suggests the next few lines as you type. Chat assistants answer questions and draft code in a side panel. AI-first editors like Cursor put the model into the editing surface so it can read and change files in context. Command-line agents like Claude Code, the Codex CLI, and Aider run a loop in your terminal, editing files and running commands toward a goal.

The axis that matters is how much the tool does between your reviews. Completion changes a line; a CLI agent can change many files and run tests before it stops. More autonomy means more leverage and a larger blast radius, so the right tool depends on how well-specified the task is and how reversible its effects are.

Pick by fit, not hype. Use completion and chat for small, local edits and learning. Use an AI-first editor or a CLI agent for multi-file changes where you can review a diff and run tests. Whatever the tool, the steering skills are the same: specify, work in small units, and verify.
key_points: Sort coding tools by interaction model, not brand.
Categories: inline completion, chat assistants, AI-first editors, CLI agents.
The key axis is how much the tool does between your reviews.
More autonomy means more leverage and a larger blast radius; match it to the task.

links.title: Cursor Documentation
links.note: An AI-first editor that put the model in the editing surface.

links.title: Aider Documentation
links.note: A command-line AI pair programmer that edits files in Git.

links.title: Claude Code: Common Workflows
links.note: Operational patterns for a CLI coding agent.

flashcards.front: What is the useful axis for sorting coding tools?
flashcards.back: How much they do between your reviews, from one line to many files.

flashcards.front: Name the four interaction categories.
flashcards.back: Inline completion, chat assistants, AI-first editors, and CLI agents.

flashcards.front: What does more tool autonomy bring?
flashcards.back: More leverage and a larger blast radius, so match it to the task.

quiz.q: What most distinguishes a CLI coding agent from inline completion?
choices: It cannot edit files.
It uses a different programming language.
It can change many files and run commands in a loop before stopping.
It never makes mistakes.
quiz.explain: The difference is how much it does autonomously between your reviews.

quiz.q: How should you choose a coding tool?
choices: By which has the most features.
Always pick the most autonomous one.
By which is newest.
By fit: match the tool's autonomy to how well-specified and reversible the task is.
quiz.explain: Fit to the task beats hype; autonomy should match specification and reversibility.

exercise.prompt: You need one small local edit and want to learn as you go. Which tool tier fits best?
choices: None; do it without any AI.
A fully autonomous CLI agent that edits many files.
Inline completion or a chat assistant for a small, local change.
A multi-agent swarm.
exercise.explain: Match tool autonomy to the task; a small local edit does not need a multi-file agent.

outcome: You can categorize coding tools by interaction model and pick one to fit a task.

title: Prompt in reviewable units
summary: Good coding-agent prompts describe one change, one boundary, and one check, so the result is small enough to audit.
body_html:
A coding model is easiest to steer when the work unit is small enough to review without archaeology. Name the file or surface, state the behavior change, list the non-goals, and ask for the verification command. That prompt shape gives the model room to help without letting it silently redefine the task.

Small units compound. A change you can read in one sitting is a change you can actually verify, and a series of small verified changes is far safer than one large opaque diff. This is the same logic as small commits in ordinary engineering: each step is reversible and each step is checkable.

Non-goals do quiet but important work. Telling the model what not to touch protects nearby code from opportunistic edits, which is one of the most common ways an AI change grows beyond what you asked for. State the boundary and the model stays inside it.
key_points: One prompt should map to one reviewable change.
Name the file, the behavior change, the non-goals, and the verification.
Small verified steps beat one large opaque diff.
Non-goals protect nearby code from opportunistic edits.

links.title: Claude Code: Common Workflows
links.note: Patterns for scoping coding-agent work into reviewable units.

links.title: Aider Documentation
links.note: Editing in small, Git-tracked steps with an AI pair programmer.

flashcards.front: What four things should a coding prompt name?
flashcards.back: The file or surface, the behavior change, the non-goals, and the verification.

flashcards.front: Why prefer small units?
flashcards.back: A change you can read in one sitting is one you can actually verify and revert.

flashcards.front: What do non-goals protect against?
flashcards.back: Opportunistic edits to nearby code that grow the change beyond what you asked.

quiz.q: Why map one prompt to one reviewable change?
choices: Because small, checkable steps are easier to verify and revert than a large opaque diff.
Because models cannot make big changes.
To avoid using tools.
To use more tokens.
quiz.explain: Reviewability is the goal; small units make it achievable.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of stating non-goals in a coding prompt?
choices: To make the prompt longer.
To protect nearby code from opportunistic, unrequested edits.
To confuse the model.
To raise the temperature.
quiz.explain: Boundaries keep the change inside the scope you intended.

exercise.goal: Ask for a small, reviewable change that adds a timeout to an existing auth token refresh call.
choices: In auth/refresh.js, add a 5 second timeout to the token refresh request only. Do not change storage or login flow. Add or update one test that proves timeout rejection.
Rewrite the auth module with better error handling.
Improve auth and clean up anything nearby.
exercise.explain: The best rewrite names the file, boundary, behavior, non-goals, and check.

outcome: You can shape a coding-agent request so the result is small enough to audit.

title: Prompting for code that fits
summary: Specific prompts that carry context, constraints, and examples produce code that fits your codebase instead of generic boilerplate.
body_html:
The difference between a useful patch and generic boilerplate is usually the prompt. A model fills gaps with the most common pattern it knows, which may not match your conventions, libraries, or constraints. The fix is to supply the context it cannot infer: the relevant file or signature, the libraries and style to use, and a concrete example of the shape you want.

Three habits raise quality fast. State the contract precisely: inputs, outputs, and edge cases, not just a vague goal. Show a short example of existing code so the model matches your conventions. And name the constraints: the version, the dependencies it may or may not add, and the performance or security requirements that matter.

Ask for the check, not just the change. Requesting the test or the verification command alongside the code turns a plausible draft into something you can immediately confirm. A prompt that ends with how will we know this works gets you both the implementation and the acceptance criteria in one pass.
key_points: Models fill gaps with generic patterns; supply the context they cannot infer.
State the contract: inputs, outputs, and edge cases, not a vague goal.
Show a short example so the model matches your conventions and libraries.
Ask for the test or verification alongside the code.

links.title: Anthropic: Prompt Engineering Overview
links.note: Concrete techniques: context, examples, and constraints.

links.title: OpenAI: Prompt Engineering
links.note: Actionable tactics for clearer model outputs.

links.title: DAIR.AI Prompting Guide
links.note: A broad compendium of prompting techniques.

flashcards.front: Why does a model produce generic boilerplate?
flashcards.back: It fills gaps with the most common pattern unless you supply your context.

flashcards.front: What three things sharpen a code prompt?
flashcards.back: A precise contract, a short example of your conventions, and explicit constraints.

flashcards.front: What should a strong code prompt also request?
flashcards.back: The test or verification, so you get acceptance criteria with the code.

quiz.q: Your prompts keep producing code that ignores your conventions. Best fix?
choices: Switch to a smaller model.
Raise the temperature.
Include a short example of existing code and name the libraries and style to use.
Ask for more code at once.
quiz.explain: A concrete example and named constraints give the model your context to match.

quiz.q: Why ask for the test alongside the implementation?
choices: It makes the model slower.
It replaces the need to read the code.
Tests are required by law.
It turns a plausible draft into something you can immediately verify.
quiz.explain: Requesting the check yields the acceptance criteria in the same pass.

exercise.weak_prompt: Write a function to validate emails.
exercise.goal: Ask for a small, testable function with the exact rule, the edge cases, and tests specified.
choices: Write a function to validate emails. Make it robust and production ready.

In validators.py, add is_valid_email(s) that returns True only when s has exactly one @ with non-empty local and domain parts and at least one dot in the domain, and rejects leading or trailing dots. Add tests for a valid address, a missing @, and a trailing dot.
Add full RFC 5322 email validation using one large regular expression.
exercise.explain: The strong prompt names the file, the exact rule, the edge cases, and the tests, so the result is small and checkable.

outcome: You can write code prompts that carry context, constraints, and a built-in check.

title: Plan first, then build
summary: For non-trivial changes, agreeing on a short plan with the model before it writes code prevents large wrong turns.
body_html:
For anything beyond a small edit, ask the model to propose a short plan before it writes code. A plan is cheap to read and cheap to change; a finished implementation built on a wrong assumption is expensive to unwind. Reviewing the plan first is where you catch a misread requirement while it still costs one sentence to fix.

A good plan names the files it will touch, the order of changes, and the verification at the end. Reading it tells you whether the model understood the task and lets you correct course before any code exists. This is the same instinct as designing before implementing, applied to a collaborator that builds very fast.

Plan-first also keeps large tasks reviewable. Once you agree on the steps, you can have the model execute them one at a time, checking each before the next. That converts a single intimidating diff into a sequence of small verified changes, which is exactly the unit you want.
key_points: Ask for a short plan before code on any non-trivial change.
A plan is cheap to fix; an implementation on a wrong assumption is not.
A good plan names files, order of changes, and the final verification.
Agreeing on steps lets you execute and check them one at a time.

links.title: Claude Code: Common Workflows
links.note: Plan-then-execute patterns for agent-driven coding.

links.title: Anthropic: Prompt Engineering Overview
links.note: Asking a model to reason and plan before acting.

flashcards.front: Why plan before coding with a model?
flashcards.back: A plan is cheap to fix; a wrong implementation is expensive to unwind.

flashcards.front: What does a good plan name?
flashcards.back: The files to touch, the order of changes, and the final verification.

flashcards.front: How does plan-first keep big tasks reviewable?
flashcards.back: You execute the agreed steps one at a time, checking each before the next.

quiz.q: What is the main benefit of reviewing a plan before code exists?
choices: You catch a misread requirement while it still costs a sentence to fix.
It removes the need for tests.
It guarantees correct code.
It makes the model write faster.
quiz.explain: Correcting a plan is far cheaper than unwinding a wrong implementation.

quiz.q: How does plan-first convert an intimidating change into a safe one?
choices: By doing it all in one diff.
By turning agreed steps into a sequence of small, verified changes.
By skipping verification.
By using a larger model.
quiz.explain: Stepwise execution against an agreed plan keeps each change reviewable.

exercise.goal: Get a short plan before any code so you can catch a wrong assumption early.
choices: Rewrite the whole auth system your way.
Refactor the auth module and make it cleaner.
Propose a short plan first: the files you will touch, the order of changes, and the test you will run; wait for my approval before editing.
exercise.explain: Reviewing the plan first catches a misread requirement while it still costs a sentence to fix.

outcome: You can use a plan-first loop to keep non-trivial AI changes on track.

title: Trust but verify AI-written code
summary: Treat model output as a useful draft, then prove the contract yourself before accepting it.
body_html:
AI-written code is a hypothesis about your codebase, not evidence that the change is correct. The model may know the local symbols, but it does not own the product contract, the edge cases, or the release risk. Your job is to convert a plausible patch into a checked change.

The fastest verification loop is simple: restate the contract, inspect the data flow, run the narrowest useful tests, and add a missing test when the risk is real. Ask the model to explain a suspicious line only after you have formed your own question. Otherwise the answer can sound coherent while quietly moving your standard of proof.

For generated code, review the boundaries first. Check inputs, outputs, null cases, permissions, persistence, retries, time, and cleanup. Then look for the model's favorite failure mode: solving the example while missing the invariant. A passing happy-path test is useful, but it is not acceptance.
key_points: Accept the model's speed, not its authority.
Restate the contract before reading the patch so you know what evidence you need.
Run focused tests and add one when the likely failure is not covered.
Use the model for explanation and alternatives, but keep final judgment outside the model.

links.title: GitHub Docs: Use Copilot Code Review
links.note: A concrete reference point for AI-assisted code review flows.

links.title: Software Engineering at Google: Testing Overview
links.note: Why automated tests make repeated change safer.

links.title: OpenAI: Prompt Engineering

flashcards.front: What status should AI-written code have before review?
flashcards.back: A plausible draft or hypothesis, not evidence of correctness.

flashcards.front: What should you restate before inspecting a generated patch?
flashcards.back: The contract: inputs, outputs, invariants, and acceptance checks.

flashcards.front: Why ask the model for an explanation after forming your own question?
flashcards.back: It keeps the model from setting the standard of proof with a fluent story.

flashcards.front: What is the common generated-code failure mode in this lesson?
flashcards.back: Solving the example while missing the invariant.

quiz.q: A generated function passes the sample case. What is the next best move?
choices: Ask the model whether the code is correct and accept a confident answer.
Rewrite the whole module manually before looking at the patch.
Check the contract and run or add a focused test for the likely edge case.
Accept the patch because the model already saw the repository.
quiz.explain: The sample case is a clue, not acceptance. Verify the contract and the risky edge.

quiz.q: Which review question is most useful for AI-written code?
choices: Could a larger model have written it faster?
Does the code look idiomatic?
Did the model explain it fluently?
What invariant could this patch violate while still passing the happy path?
quiz.explain: Generated code often handles the visible example while missing the invariant.

quiz.q: When should you ask the model to explain a suspicious line?
choices: After you have a specific question or hypothesis to test.
Only after merging, so you do not slow down.
Never, because explanations from models cannot be useful.
Before reading the code, so it can frame the review.
quiz.explain: A specific question lets the explanation support your review instead of replacing it.

exercise.prompt: Cart items have priceCents and quantity. This AI-written helper passes one-item smoke tests but has one bug. Click the faulty line.
exercise.explain: The reducer ignores quantity, so it charges one unit no matter how many the cart contains.

outcome: You can turn a plausible generated patch into a checked change with explicit acceptance evidence.

title: Debugging with AI
summary: AI is strongest at debugging when you give it the evidence and a hypothesis, not just the symptom.
body_html:
Pasting an error and asking what is wrong often gets a confident guess. AI debugging works far better when you hand the model the same evidence a good engineer would gather: the exact error, the smallest input that reproduces it, the relevant code, and what you expected versus what happened. With evidence, the model reasons; without it, it speculates.

Drive the model toward a hypothesis, then test it. Ask what conditions could produce this error, get a short list of candidates, and check the most likely one against the actual data flow. This keeps you debugging the real cause rather than applying the first plausible patch, which often hides the symptom while leaving the bug.

Stay the judge of the fix. A change that makes the error disappear is not the same as a change that fixes the cause. Confirm the fix addresses the mechanism you identified, and add a test that would have caught the bug, so it cannot return silently.
key_points: Give the model evidence: the error, a minimal repro, the code, and expected versus actual.
Drive toward hypotheses, then test the most likely against the data flow.
A vanished symptom is not a fixed cause.
Add a regression test that would have caught the bug.

links.title: Claude Code: Common Workflows
links.note: Patterns for debugging with a coding agent.

links.title: Software Engineering at Google: Testing Overview
links.note: Why a regression test makes a fix durable.

flashcards.front: What evidence should you give an AI to debug well?
flashcards.back: The exact error, a minimal repro, the relevant code, and expected versus actual.

flashcards.front: Why is a vanished symptom not enough?
flashcards.back: A fix can hide the symptom while leaving the underlying cause in place.

flashcards.front: What confirms a debugging fix is durable?
flashcards.back: A regression test that would have caught the original bug.

quiz.q: Why does pasting only the error message produce weak AI debugging?
choices: Models cannot read errors.
Without a repro, the code, and expected behavior, the model speculates instead of reasoning.
Error messages are always wrong.
It uses too many tokens.
quiz.explain: Evidence turns guessing into reasoning about the actual cause.

quiz.q: An AI fix makes the error disappear. What should you confirm?
choices: That the model is confident.
Nothing; a gone error means done.
That the fix addresses the identified cause, then add a regression test.
That the code is shorter.
quiz.explain: Fixing the mechanism and adding a test prevents a silent return.

exercise.prompt: This AI-written median function works on some inputs but is wrong on [1, 2, 10]. Click the faulty line.
exercise.explain: Array.sort() with no comparator sorts as strings, so [1, 2, 10] becomes [1, 10, 2], and it also mutates the caller's array. Use sort((a, b) => a - b) on a copy. A complete median should also average the two middle values on even-length input.

outcome: You can debug with AI by supplying evidence and verifying the cause, not the symptom.

title: Reading AI-written code
summary: Reading generated code is a distinct skill: it is fluent and plausible, so you must read for intent and invariants, not just style.
body_html:
AI-written code reads well. It is idiomatic, well-named, and confident, which is exactly why it can lull a reviewer. The danger is not ugly code you would catch on sight; it is clean code that quietly does the wrong thing. Reading generated code is its own skill, and it leans on intent and invariants rather than surface style.

Read against the contract, not the prose. For each function ask what it must guarantee, then check whether the code actually guarantees it on the edges: empty inputs, units and scales, boundary values, error paths, and shared state. The classic generated bug satisfies the obvious case while violating a quieter invariant that the happy path never exercises.

Be most skeptical where the code is most confident. Comments that assert a fact, a value that looks plausible, or a default that fills a gap all deserve a check against the real data. Fluency is not correctness, and the better the code looks, the more deliberately it earns a read.
key_points: Generated code is fluent and plausible, which can lull a reviewer.
Read against the contract and invariants, not surface style.
Check edges: empty inputs, units and scale, boundaries, error paths, shared state.
Be most skeptical where the code is most confident.

links.title: Software Engineering at Google: Testing Overview
links.note: Invariants and tests as the backstop for review.

links.title: GitHub Docs: Use Copilot Code Review
links.note: Workflows for reviewing AI-assisted changes.

flashcards.front: Why is reading AI code its own skill?
flashcards.back: It is fluent and plausible, so the risk is clean code that quietly does the wrong thing.

flashcards.front: What should you read generated code against?
flashcards.back: The contract and its invariants, especially on the edges, not surface style.

flashcards.front: Where should you be most skeptical?
flashcards.back: Where the code is most confident: asserting comments, plausible values, gap-filling defaults.

quiz.q: What is the real danger when reviewing AI-written code?
choices: Code with too many comments.
Code that is too short.
Obviously ugly code you catch on sight.
Clean, fluent code that quietly violates an invariant the happy path never exercises.
quiz.explain: Fluency masks the quiet invariant violation, which is the hard case to catch.

quiz.q: What should drive your reading of generated code?
choices: Whether it actually guarantees the contract on the edges.
How long it is.
How confident the comments sound.
Whether it looks idiomatic.
quiz.explain: Edges and invariants, not style, decide correctness.

exercise.prompt: A comment claims percent is a whole number like 20 for 20%. The function reads cleanly but is wrong. Click the faulty line.
lines: # percent is a whole number, e.g. 20 for 20 percent off
exercise.explain: Since percent is a whole number, the formula must divide by 100. As written it computes 50 - 50*20, which is far below zero. The fluent comment hid the missing /100.

outcome: You can read generated code for intent and invariants instead of being lulled by its fluency.

title: Knowing when to take the wheel
summary: Expert vibe coding is calibrated delegation: knowing which work to hand the model and which work to do yourself.
body_html:
The expert skill is not maximal automation; it is calibrated delegation. You learn which work the model does reliably and which work it does plausibly but poorly, and you route accordingly. Boilerplate, mechanical refactors, test scaffolding, and well-specified functions are strong hand-offs. Subtle concurrency, security-sensitive logic, novel architecture, and anything where the cost of a quiet error is high are work to keep close.

Watch for the loop where you keep nudging the model toward a fix it cannot quite reach. Past a couple of failed attempts, the fastest path is usually to take the wheel: write the hard part yourself, then hand the routine parts back. Recognizing that moment early saves more time than one more reprompt.

Calibration comes from keeping score honestly. Notice where AI-written changes later caused incidents and where they saved real time, and adjust what you delegate. The goal is a durable division of labor: the model for leverage on the routine, you for judgment on the consequential.
key_points: Expertise is calibrated delegation, not maximal automation.
Hand off boilerplate, mechanical refactors, and well-specified work.
Keep concurrency, security, novel architecture, and high-cost-of-error work close.
After a couple of failed reprompts, take the wheel; recognizing that moment saves time.

links.title: Anthropic: Building Effective Agents
links.note: Matching autonomy to task is the same calibration here.

links.title: Karpathy: Vibe Coding MenuGen
links.note: An honest account of where vibe coding helped and where it did not.

flashcards.front: What is the expert vibe-coding skill?
flashcards.back: Calibrated delegation: routing work to the model or to yourself based on reliability.

flashcards.front: What work should stay close to the human?
flashcards.back: Concurrency, security-sensitive logic, novel architecture, and high-cost-of-error work.

flashcards.front: When should you take the wheel?
flashcards.back: After a couple of failed reprompts on a fix the model cannot quite reach.

quiz.q: What best describes expert-level vibe coding?
choices: Automating as much as technically possible.
Calibrated delegation: routing routine work to the model and consequential work to yourself.
Never using AI for code.
Always rewriting the model's output.
quiz.explain: The skill is judgment about what to delegate, not the amount of automation.

quiz.q: You have reprompted three times and the model still cannot fix a subtle bug. Best move?
choices: Switch to a larger model and retry indefinitely.
Reprompt five more times.
Take the wheel: write the hard part yourself, then hand back the routine parts.
Accept the broken code.
quiz.explain: Recognizing the unreachable-fix loop early and taking over saves the most time.

exercise.prompt: You have reprompted three times and the model still cannot fix a subtle concurrency bug. Best move?
choices: Switch models and retry indefinitely.
Reprompt five more times.
Take the wheel: write the hard part yourself, then hand back the routine parts.
Ship the broken code.

outcome: You can decide what to delegate to a coding model and when to take over yourself.

title: Shipping AI features responsibly: privacy, copyright, fairness
summary: Moving fast with AI does not suspend the rules. Three obligations follow your code into production: protect personal data, respect copyright, and watch for unfair outcomes.
body_html:
When you ship a feature built with an AI collaborator, you own its consequences, not the model. Privacy comes first because it is the easiest to get wrong by accident. Personal data (names, emails, payment details, anything that identifies someone) should be collected only when needed, logged carefully, and never pasted into a third-party model or analytics tool without a basis for doing so. A common vibe-coding bug is logging a whole request object, including a password or a token, to an analytics pipeline. In Canada, PIPEDA governs how private-sector organizations handle personal information; equivalents exist in most jurisdictions, and they apply to AI features exactly as to any other code.

Copyright reaches both inputs and outputs. Base-model training-data provenance is mainly a provider and vendor-diligence question, but you still own your own inputs, outputs, licenses, and usage, so how you use generated output is yours: do not ship code or text whose license you have not checked, and do not feed proprietary or confidential material into a tool whose terms permit reuse. Generated code can reproduce licensed snippets, so treat unfamiliar output the way you would treat a stranger's code: review and attribute where required.

Fairness is the quiet one. A feature can work on average and still fail a subgroup, because the data it learned from underrepresents them. You catch this by measuring outcomes per group, not just in aggregate, and by asking who is harmed when the system is wrong. None of this slows a vibe-coding loop much; it is a short checklist (what personal data does this touch, whose license applies, who could this treat unfairly) run before you ship, not after a complaint.
key_points: You own the consequences of an AI-built feature; collect and log personal data minimally and deliberately.
Never paste secrets or PII into a third-party model or analytics tool without a basis.
Check the license of generated output and of anything you feed in; generated code can reproduce licensed snippets.
Measure outcomes per subgroup, not just on average, to catch fairness failures.

links.title: PIPEDA (Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada)
links.note: Canada's private-sector privacy law; it applies to AI features like any other code.

links.title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework
links.note: A practical framework for governing AI risk, including privacy and fairness.

flashcards.front: What is the most common accidental privacy bug in vibe-coded features?
flashcards.back: Logging a whole request object (including a password, token, or PII) into analytics or logs that were not built to hold secrets.

flashcards.front: Whose problem is the license of AI-generated code you ship?
flashcards.back: Yours. Generated output can reproduce licensed snippets, so review and check the license before shipping it.

flashcards.front: How do you catch a fairness failure that aggregate metrics hide?
flashcards.back: Measure outcomes per subgroup and ask who is harmed when the system is wrong, rather than trusting the average.

quiz.q: An AI-suggested handler logs the full signup request to your analytics tool. The risk is:
choices: Slower analytics
Better debugging only
None; logs are private
Personal data and secrets (like the password) leak into a system not built to hold them
quiz.explain: Logging the whole request ships PII and credentials into a third-party pipeline. Log deliberately and redact secrets.

quiz.q: You accept a block of AI-generated code that closely matches a known GPL project. Before shipping you should:
choices: Check the license implications, since generated code can reproduce licensed snippets, and rewrite or comply
Add a comment crediting the AI
Nothing changes
Ship it; AI output has no license
quiz.explain: Generated output can reproduce licensed code. The obligation to check and comply is yours, not the model's.

quiz.q: A loan-screening helper has 92% accuracy overall. What still must you check?
choices: Nothing; 92% is enough
Whether accuracy and error costs are similar across subgroups, since the average can hide harm to an underrepresented group
Only the training time
The model's parameter count
quiz.explain: Aggregate accuracy can mask a subgroup the data underrepresents. Measure per group and ask who is harmed by errors.

exercise.prompt: This AI-suggested signup handler has a serious privacy and security bug. Which line is it?
exercise.explain: Line 4 ships the plaintext password and the email (PII) into a third-party analytics pipeline. Never log secrets or personal data to analytics; track an anonymous event id instead.

outcome: Run a short privacy, copyright, and fairness check before shipping an AI-built feature, and spot a PII leak in review.

title: Operating AI features: pipelines, deployment, monitoring
summary: Shipping the code is the start, not the end. AI features need a lifecycle: validated data in, gated deploys out, and monitoring that catches silent drift.
body_html:
Traditional software is mostly tested before release and then runs as written. AI features are different: their behavior depends on data and on a model that the world keeps changing around. The discipline for managing that is usually called MLOps, and the core idea is a loop rather than a launch. It starts upstream of any model, with data: validate and version your inputs, because a silent change in the data feeding a feature is one of the most common ways production AI degrades without an error ever being thrown.

Deployment should be gated, not flipped. Roll a new model or prompt out to a small slice of traffic first (a canary), compare it against the current version on live data, and promote only if it holds up. This is the production analogue of the train, dev, test split: you never bet the whole system on an estimate. Keep the previous version one switch away so a rollback is instant.

Monitoring is what makes the loop honest. Track input distributions and output quality over time, not just uptime, because the dangerous failures are silent: the model keeps responding, but its inputs have drifted away from what it learned, and quality slips. Set triggers (a drift threshold, a rising error or complaint rate) that prompt a retrain or rollback. The famous lesson from production ML is that the model code is a small part of a real system; the data pipelines, monitoring, and glue around it are where the ongoing cost and risk live.
key_points: AI behavior depends on data and a shifting world, so it needs a lifecycle loop, not a one-time launch.
Validate and version input data; silent data changes are a top cause of quiet degradation.
Deploy behind a gate (canary or staged rollout) with the previous version one switch away.
Monitor input drift and output quality, not just uptime; set triggers for retrain or rollback.

links.title: Google Cloud: MLOps continuous delivery and automation
links.note: The reference picture of the ML lifecycle and automation levels.

links.title: ml-ops.org: MLOps principles
links.note: Versioning, testing, monitoring, and reproducibility for ML systems.

flashcards.front: Why is an AI feature a loop rather than a one-time launch?
flashcards.back: Its behavior depends on data and a model the world keeps changing around, so it needs continuous validation, gated deploys, and monitoring.

flashcards.front: What is a canary deploy and why use it for a new model?
flashcards.back: Routing a small slice of live traffic to the new version first and comparing it to the current one, promoting only if it holds. It is the production train/dev/test.

flashcards.front: What kind of production failure does uptime monitoring miss?
flashcards.back: Silent drift: the model keeps responding, but inputs have moved away from training and quality quietly slips. You need input and output monitoring.

quiz.q: A deployed classifier slowly gets worse with no errors in the logs. The most likely cause is:
choices: Too many unit tests
A syntax error
Data drift: live inputs have moved away from what the model learned
The server is down
quiz.explain: Silent quality loss with no errors is the signature of drift. Uptime is fine; the input distribution changed.

quiz.q: The safest way to roll out a new model version is:
choices: Only in development
Whenever the model is retrained, automatically to all users
Replace the old one everywhere at once
A canary or staged rollout compared on live traffic, with instant rollback to the previous version
quiz.explain: Gate the rollout and keep rollback one switch away. Never bet the whole system on an unverified version.

quiz.q: The classic production-ML lesson about where cost and risk live is:
choices: In the surrounding data pipelines, monitoring, and glue, which dwarf the model code
In the choice of programming language
In the number of GPUs
In the model code, which dominates the system
quiz.explain: Model code is a small fraction of a real ML system; the data and operational scaffolding carry most of the ongoing cost and risk.

exercise.prompt: Put the lifecycle of a deployed AI feature in the order it should run.
items: Validate and version the input data before anything depends on it
Train or configure the model and evaluate it against a baseline on held-out data
Deploy behind a gate (canary or staged rollout), comparing against the current version
Monitor live inputs and outputs for drift and quality regressions
Retrain or roll back when a monitored trigger fires, then repeat
exercise.explain: Data first, then evaluate against a baseline, then gated deploy, then monitor, then act on triggers. It is a loop: the last step feeds the first.

outcome: Lay out the lifecycle of a deployed AI feature: validated data, gated rollout, drift monitoring, and trigger-driven retrain or rollback.

sources.claim: Model code is a small fraction of a real ML system; data and operational scaffolding dominate.
sources.title: Sculley et al., Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems (2015)

title: Capstone: review an AI-generated patch
summary: The whole vibe-coding discipline in one task: read an AI-written diff, find the real bug, notice the missing test, catch the security hole, and make a merge call.
body_html:
The leverage in vibe coding is not typing speed; it is review. An AI can produce a plausible patch in seconds, and plausible is exactly the danger, because it reads as finished while hiding the failure modes you are paid to catch. This capstone hands you a small, realistic AI-generated patch and walks the review the way a careful engineer does: identify the most serious defect, check what test should exist and does not, name the security issue, and decide whether to merge, request changes, or reject.

There is one defensible answer per step, with the reasoning revealed after you choose. The habit being built is reading AI output as a proposal to be verified, not an answer to be accepted.
key_points: Review is where vibe coding earns its safety; plausible code is the trap.
String-interpolated SQL is an injection hole; parameterized queries are the fix.
A patch without a test for its risky path is incomplete, even if it looks correct.
Merge, request changes, or reject is a real decision; default to request changes when a security issue is open.

links.title: OWASP: SQL Injection
links.note: What injection is and why parameterized queries prevent it.

flashcards.front: Why is plausible AI-generated code dangerous?
flashcards.back: It reads as finished, which lowers your guard, while it can still hide bugs, missing tests, and security holes that only review catches.

flashcards.front: The fix for string-built SQL is:
flashcards.back: A parameterized (bound-parameter) query, plus validating the input type. Never interpolate user input into a query string.

quiz.q: An AI patch builds a SQL query with an f-string containing user input. This is:
choices: Fine if the input looks clean
A SQL injection vulnerability; use a parameterized query instead
Faster and therefore better
Only a style issue
quiz.explain: Interpolating user input into SQL is the classic injection hole. Bind parameters so the input can never change the query structure.

quiz.q: The patch has no test exercising a malicious or unexpected user_id. The right call is:
choices: Delete the existing tests
Merge; tests can come later
Request changes: the risky path needs a test before merge
Reject the whole feature permanently
quiz.explain: A risky path with no test is incomplete. Request changes and require coverage for the injection and validation behavior.

exercise.prompt: Review the AI-generated patch step by step, then make the merge call.
exercise.scenario_html:
Patch under review (AI-generated, adds an endpoint to fetch a user's orders):

+ def get_orders(user_id):
+     query = f"SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = {user_id}"
+     return db.execute(query).fetchall()

No tests were added. The PR description says "adds order lookup, works on my machine."

steps.q: What is the most serious defect in this patch?
choices: SQL injection: user_id is interpolated straight into the query string
It returns too many columns
It lacks a docstring
The function name is unclear
steps.explain: An f-string puts user input directly into the SQL, so a crafted user_id can rewrite the query. That is a critical injection vulnerability.

choices: A test that the function name is spelled correctly
A test exercising a malicious or non-integer user_id (and verifying a parameterized query), plus a normal-case test
A performance benchmark
No test is needed
steps.explain: The risky path is untested. You need a case proving injection input is handled safely and the query is parameterized, plus the happy path.

steps.q: What is the correct fix before this can merge?
choices: Rename the query variable
Add a comment warning about injection
Use a parameterized query with a bound parameter and validate that user_id is an integer
Trust the caller to sanitize user_id
steps.explain: Bind the parameter (for example db.execute("... WHERE user_id = ?", [user_id])) and validate the type. The input can then never alter the query structure.

steps.q: What is the right merge decision right now?
choices: Reject and delete the feature idea
Merge and open a ticket to fix it later
Merge; it works on their machine
Request changes: do not merge until the query is parameterized, the input validated, and a test covers the risky path
steps.explain: An open security issue means request changes, not merge-and-hope. 'Works on my machine' is not a security argument.

exercise.outcome: You caught an injection vulnerability, named the missing test, specified the parameterized fix, and made the disciplined call: request changes before merge.

outcome: Review an AI-generated patch end to end: find the real defect, the missing test, the security hole, and make a defensible merge decision.
